'''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町にある[[神社]]で、都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]]の[[一の宮]]と伝わる。都波岐神社と奈加等神社の合殿社で、「'''都波岐奈加等神社'''」とひっくるめて呼ばれることが多いほど実質的にひとつの神社となっているが、祭神は別である。鎮座は[[雄略天皇]]23年（[[479年]]）、勅により造高雄束命が創建と伝わる。両神社とも、[[延喜式]]においては小社、明治以降の社格は[[県社]]。
== 祭神 == :'''都波岐神社''' * [[猿田彦大神]]（さるたひこのおおかみ） :'''奈加等神社''' * [[天椹野命]]（あまのくののみこと、中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖） * [[中筒之男命]]（なかつつおのみこと）
== 主な年中行事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日）
== 交通 == * [[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[関西本線]]または[[伊勢鉄道]][[河原田駅]]下車、三重交通バスで「高岡」バス停まで。徒歩10分。 * [[伊勢鉄道]][[鈴鹿市駅]]下車、三重交通バスで「高岡」バス停まで。徒歩10分。 * [[国道23号]][[鈴鹿大橋]]から南約1ｋｍの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く。
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/jt024.htm 三重県の開運神社仏閣]

[[Category:三重県の神社|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]] [[Category:一宮|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]] [[Category:式内小社|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]] [[Category:府県社|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]]

{{神社| 名称=都波岐神社・奈加等神社| 所在地=三重県鈴鹿市一ノ宮町1181| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|54|04.5|N|136|36|04.5|E|scale:30000}}| 祭神=（都波岐神社）猿田彦大神
（奈加等神社）天椹野命・中筒之男命| 社格=式内社（小）・伊勢国一宮・県社| 創建=[[雄略天皇]]23年（[[479年]]）| 例祭=[[10月10日]]}} '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]である。両社とも[[式内社]]で、旧[[社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 明治時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、「'''都波岐奈加等神社'''」とも称される。都波岐神社は[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]（さるたひこのおおかみ）、奈加等神社は中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖の[[天椹野命]]（あまのくののみこと）と[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]（なかつつおのみこと）を祀る。 ==歴史== 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年（[[479年]]）、勅により造高雄束命が現在地の伊勢国河曲郡中跡村に2つの神社を造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが始まりと伝える。[[延喜式神名帳]]では両社とも小社に列している。[[天長]]年間に[[弘法大師]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを奉納した。[[承暦]]3年、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より宸筆の勅額が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際、兵火で社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物によって再建された。 明治時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として県社に列格した。現在は2つの神社の祭神が1つの社殿に相殿に祀られている。 == 主な年中行事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 交通 == * [[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[関西本線]]または[[伊勢鉄道]][[河原田駅]]下車、三重交通バスで「高岡」バス停まで。徒歩10分。 * [[伊勢鉄道]][[鈴鹿市駅]]下車、三重交通バスで「高岡」バス停まで。徒歩10分。 * [[国道23号]][[鈴鹿大橋]]から南約1ｋｍの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く。 == 外部リンク == * [http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/jt024.htm 三重県の開運神社仏閣] [[Category:三重県の神社|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]] [[Category:一宮|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]] [[Category:式内小社|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]] [[Category:府県社|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]]

{{神社| 名称=都波岐神社・奈加等神社| 所在地=三重県鈴鹿市一ノ宮町1181| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|54|04.5|N|136|36|04.5|E|scale:30000}}| 祭神=（都波岐神社）猿田彦大神
（奈加等神社）天椹野命・中筒之男命| 社格=式内社（小）・伊勢国一宮・県社| 創建=[[雄略天皇]]23年（[[479年]]）| 例祭=[[10月10日]]}} '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]である。両社とも[[式内社]]で、旧[[社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 明治時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、「'''都波岐奈加等神社'''」とも称される。都波岐神社は[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]（さるたひこのおおかみ）、奈加等神社は中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖の[[天椹野命]]（あまのくののみこと）と[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]（なかつつおのみこと）を祀る。 ==歴史== 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年（[[479年]]）、勅により造高雄束命（[[猿田彦]]の八世の孫という）が現在地の伊勢国河曲郡中跡村に2つの神社を造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが始まりと伝える。[[延喜式神名帳]]では両社とも小社に列している。[[天長]]年間に[[弘法大師]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを奉納した。[[承暦]]3年、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より宸筆の勅額が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際、兵火で社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物によって再建された。 明治時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として県社に列格した。現在は2つの神社の祭神が1つの社殿に相殿に祀られている。 == 主な年中行事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 交通 == * [[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[関西本線]]または[[伊勢鉄道]][[河原田駅]]下車、三重交通バスで「高岡」バス停まで。徒歩10分。 * [[伊勢鉄道]][[鈴鹿市駅]]下車、三重交通バスで「高岡」バス停まで。徒歩10分。 * [[国道23号]][[鈴鹿大橋]]から南約1ｋｍの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く。 == 外部リンク == * [http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/jt024.htm 三重県の開運神社仏閣] [[Category:三重県の神社|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]] [[Category:一宮|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]] [[Category:式内小社|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]] [[Category:府県社|つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]]

{{神社| 名称=都波岐神社・奈加等神社| 所在地=三重県鈴鹿市一ノ宮町1181| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|54|04.5|N|136|36|04.5|E|scale:30000}}| 祭神=（都波岐神社）猿田彦大神
（奈加等神社）天椹野命・中筒之男命| 社格=式内社（小）・伊勢国一宮・県社| 創建=[[雄略天皇]]23年（[[479年]]）| 例祭=[[10月10日]]}} '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]である。両社とも[[式内社]]で、旧[[社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 明治時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、「'''都波岐奈加等神社'''」とも称される。都波岐神社は[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]（さるたひこのおおかみ）、奈加等神社は中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖の[[天椹野命]]（あまのくののみこと）と[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]（なかつつおのみこと）を祀る。 ==歴史== 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年（[[479年]]）、勅により造高雄束命（[[猿田彦]]の八世の孫という）が現在地の[[伊勢国]][[河曲郡]][[中跡村]]に2つの神社を造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが始まりと伝える。[[延喜式神名帳]]では両社とも小社に列している。[[天長]]年間に[[弘法大師]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを奉納した。[[承暦]]3年、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より[[宸翰|宸筆]]の勅額が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際、兵火で社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物によって再建された。 明治時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として[[県社]]に列格した。現在は2つの神社の[[祭神]]が1つの社殿に[[相殿]]に祀られている。 == 主な年中行事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 交通 == * [[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[関西本線]]または[[伊勢鉄道]][[河原田駅]]下車、三重交通バスで「高岡」バス停まで。徒歩10分。 * [[伊勢鉄道]][[鈴鹿市駅]]下車、三重交通バスで「高岡」バス停まで。徒歩10分。 * [[国道23号]][[鈴鹿大橋]]から南約1kmの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く。 == 関連項目 == *[[椿大神社]] *[[猿田彦神社]] *[[白鬚神社]] *[[神社一覧]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/jt024.htm 三重県の開運神社仏閣] {{DEFAULTSORT:つはきしんしやなかとしんしや}} [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:式内小社]] [[Category:府県社]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市]]

revision.comment: 諸国一宮の体裁整備。問題があれば修正をお願いいたします。

奈加等神社：天椹野命、[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]] |社格 = [[式内社]]（小2社）
[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]
旧[[県社]] |創建 = （伝）[[雄略天皇]]23年（[[479年]]） |例祭 = [[10月10日]] }} [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|230px|鳥居]] '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]。両社とも[[式内社]]で、[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 明治時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、'''都波岐奈加等神社'''とも称される。 == 祭神 == ; 都波岐神社 * '''[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]''' （さるたひこのおおかみ） ; 奈加等神社 * '''天椹野命''' （あまのくののみこと） - 中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖 * '''[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]''' （なかつつおのみこと） == 歴史 == 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年（[[479年]]）、勅により造高雄束命（[[猿田彦]]の八世の孫という）が現在地の[[伊勢国]][[河曲郡]][[中跡村]]に2つの神社を造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが始まりと伝える。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』では両社とも小社に列している。[[天長]]年間に[[弘法大師]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを奉納した。[[承暦]]3年、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より[[宸翰|宸筆]]の勅額が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際、兵火で社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物によって再建された。 明治時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として[[県社]]に列格した。現在は2つの神社の[[祭神]]が1つの社殿に[[相殿]]に祀られている。 == 主な祭事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 現地情報 == ; 所在地 * [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 ; 交通アクセス {{underline|バス}} * [[河原田駅]]（[[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[関西本線]]・[[伊勢鉄道]]）または[[鈴鹿市駅]]（[[近鉄鈴鹿線]]）から、[[三重交通]]バスで「高岡」バス停下車 （徒歩10分） {{underline|車}} * [[国道23号]]鈴鹿大橋から南約1kmの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く == 関連項目 == {{commons|Category:Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ; 猿田彦大神を祀る神社 * [[椿大神社]] - 伊勢国一宮を称するもう1つの神社 * [[猿田彦神社]] * [[白鬚神社]] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つはきしんしやなかとしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市]] [[Category:式内小社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社つはきしんしやなかとしんしや]]

旧[[県社]] |創建 = （伝）[[雄略天皇]]23年 |例祭 = [[10月10日]] }} {{mapplot|136.60125|34.90125|都波岐神社・奈加等神社}} [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|鳥居]] '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]。両社とも[[式内社]]で、[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 明治時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、'''都波岐奈加等神社'''とも称される。 == 祭神 == ; 都波岐神社 * '''[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]''' （さるたひこのおおかみ） ; 奈加等神社 * '''天椹野命''' （あまのくののみこと） - 中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖 * '''[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]''' （なかつつおのみこと） == 歴史 == 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年、[[勅]]により造高雄束命[[猿田彦]]の八世の孫とされる。が[[伊勢国]][[河曲郡]][[中跡村]]（現在地）に社殿を2つ造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが起源とされる。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』では両社とも小社に列した。[[天長]]年間に[[弘法大師]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを[[奉納]]した。[[承暦]]3年（[[1079年]]）、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より[[宸翰|宸筆]]の[[勅額]]が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際に社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物が再建した。 [[明治]]時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として[[県社]]に列格。現在は2社の[[祭神]]が1つの社殿に[[祭神#主神・配神|相殿]]として祀られる。 == 主な祭事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 所在地 == * [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 == 交通アクセス == '''路線バス''' * [[河原田駅]]（[[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[関西本線]]・[[伊勢鉄道]]）または[[鈴鹿市駅]]（[[近鉄鈴鹿線]]）から、[[三重交通]]バスで「高岡」バス停下車 （徒歩10分） '''自家用車''' * [[国道23号]]鈴鹿大橋から南約1kmの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == 関連項目 == {{commons|Category:Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ; 猿田彦大神を祀る神社 * [[椿大神社]] - 伊勢国一宮を称するもう1つの神社 * [[猿田彦神社]] * [[白鬚神社]] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つはきしんしやなかとしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社1]]

{{出典の明記|date=2019年10月}} {{神社 |名称 = 都波岐神社・奈加等神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg|280px]]
社殿 |所在地 = [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|54|04.5|N|136|36|04.5|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=都波岐神社・奈加等神社}} |祭神 = 都波岐神社：[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]
奈加等神社：天椹野命、[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]] |社格 = [[式内社]]（小2社）
[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]
旧[[県社]] |創建 = （伝）[[雄略天皇]]23年 |例祭 = [[10月10日]] }} {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|34.90125|136.60125}}|caption=都波岐神社・奈加等神社|width=256}} [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|鳥居]] '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]。両社とも[[式内社]]で、[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 明治時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、'''都波岐奈加等神社'''とも称される。 == 祭神 == ; 都波岐神社 * '''[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]''' （さるたひこのおおかみ） ; 奈加等神社 * '''天椹野命''' （あまのくののみこと） - 中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖 * '''[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]''' （なかつつおのみこと） == 歴史 == 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年、[[勅]]により[[伊勢国造]]の高雄束命[[猿田彦]]の八世の孫とされる。が[[伊勢国]][[河曲郡]][[中跡村]]（現在地）に社殿を2つ造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが起源とされる。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』では両社とも小社に列した。[[天長]]年間に[[空海|弘法大師（空海）]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを[[奉納]]した。[[承暦]]3年（[[1079年]]）、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より[[宸翰|宸筆]]の[[勅額]]が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際に社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物が再建した。 [[明治]]時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として[[県社]]に列格。現在は2社の[[祭神]]が1つの社殿に[[祭神#主神・配神|相殿]]として祀られる。 == 主な祭事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 所在地 == * [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 == 交通アクセス == '''路線バス''' * [[河原田駅]]（[[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[関西本線]]・[[伊勢鉄道]]）または[[鈴鹿市駅]]（[[近鉄鈴鹿線]]）から、[[三重交通]]バスで「高岡」バス停下車 （徒歩10分） '''自家用車''' * [[国道23号]]鈴鹿大橋から南約1kmの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == 関連項目 == {{commons|Category:Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ; 猿田彦大神を祀る神社 * [[椿大神社]] - 伊勢国一宮を称するもう1つの神社 * [[猿田彦神社]] * [[白鬚神社]] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つはきしんしやなかとしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社1]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]]

{{出典の明記|date=2019年10月}} {{神社 |名称 = 都波岐神社・奈加等神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg|280px]]
社殿 |所在地 = [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|54|04.5|N|136|36|04.5|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=都波岐神社・奈加等神社}} |祭神 = 都波岐神社：[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]
奈加等神社：天椹野命、[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]] |社格 = [[式内社]]（小2社）
[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]
旧[[県社]] |創建 = （伝）[[雄略天皇]]23年 |例祭 = [[10月10日]] }} {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|34.90125|136.60125}}|caption=都波岐神社・奈加等神社|width=256}} [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|鳥居]] '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]。 == 概要 == 両社とも[[式内社]]で、[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 [[明治]]時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、'''都波岐奈加等神社'''とも称される。 == 祭神 == ; 都波岐神社 * '''[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]''' （さるたひこのおおかみ） ; 奈加等神社 * '''天椹野命''' （あまのくののみこと） - 中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖。 * '''[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]''' （なかつつおのみこと） == 歴史 == 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年、[[勅]]により[[伊勢国造]]の高雄束命[[猿田彦]]の八世の孫とされる。が[[伊勢国]][[河曲郡]][[中跡村]]（現在地）に社殿を2つ造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが起源とされる。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』では両社とも小社に列した。[[天長]]年間に[[空海|弘法大師（空海）]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを[[奉納]]した。[[承暦]]3年（[[1079年]]）、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より[[宸翰|宸筆]]の[[勅額]]が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際に社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物が再建した。 [[明治]]時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として[[県社]]に列格。現在は2社の[[祭神]]が1つの社殿に[[祭神#主神・配神|相殿]]として祀られる。 == 主な祭事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 所在地 == * [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 == 交通アクセス == ; 路線バス * [[河原田駅]]（[[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[関西線 (名古屋地区)|関西本線]]・[[伊勢鉄道]][[伊勢鉄道伊勢線|伊勢線]]）または[[鈴鹿市駅]]（[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄鈴鹿線|鈴鹿線]]）から、[[三重交通]]バスで「高岡」バス停下車、徒歩で約10分。 ; 自家用車 * [[国道23号]]鈴鹿大橋から南約1kmの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == 関連項目 == {{commons|Category:Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ; 猿田彦大神を祀る神社 * [[椿大神社]] - 伊勢国一宮を称するもう1つの神社。 * [[猿田彦神社]] * [[白鬚神社]] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つはきしんしやなかとしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社1]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Jinja
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Jinja'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie| Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were amalgamated in the [[Meiji period]]{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto]]'''| 天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|'''Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto'''| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the impesrialfamily by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Jinja'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were amalgamated in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto'''|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|'''Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto'''| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the impesrialfamily by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

{{出典の明記|date=2019年10月}} {{神社 |名称 = 都波岐神社・奈加等神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg|250px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|54|04.5|N|136|36|04.5|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=都波岐神社・奈加等神社}} |祭神 = 都波岐神社：[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]
奈加等神社：天椹野命、[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]] |社格 = [[式内社]]（小2社）
[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]
旧[[県社]] |創建 = （伝）[[雄略天皇]]23年 |例祭 = [[10月10日]] |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=4.5|lon_deg=136|lon_min=36|lon_sec=4.5|label=都波岐神社
奈加等神社|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}} }} [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|鳥居]] '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]。 == 概要 == 両社とも[[式内社]]で、[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 [[明治]]時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、'''都波岐奈加等神社'''とも称される。 == 祭神 == ; 都波岐神社 * '''[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]''' （さるたひこのおおかみ） ; 奈加等神社 * '''天椹野命''' （あまのくののみこと） - 中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖。 * '''[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]''' （なかつつおのみこと） == 歴史 == 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年、[[勅]]により[[伊勢国造]]の高雄束命[[猿田彦]]の八世の孫とされる。が[[伊勢国]][[河曲郡]][[中跡村]]（現在地）に社殿を2つ造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが起源とされる。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』では両社とも小社に列した。[[天長]]年間に[[空海|弘法大師（空海）]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを[[奉納]]した。[[承暦]]3年（[[1079年]]）、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より[[宸翰|宸筆]]の[[勅額]]が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際に社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物が再建した。 [[明治]]時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として[[県社]]に列格。現在は2社の[[祭神]]が1つの社殿に[[祭神#主神・配神|相殿]]として祀られる。 == 主な祭事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 所在地 == * [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 == 交通アクセス == ; 路線バス * [[河原田駅]]（[[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[関西線 (名古屋地区)|関西本線]]・[[伊勢鉄道]][[伊勢鉄道伊勢線|伊勢線]]）または[[鈴鹿市駅]]（[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄鈴鹿線|鈴鹿線]]）から、[[三重交通]]バスで「高岡」バス停下車、徒歩で約10分。 ; 自家用車 * [[国道23号]]鈴鹿大橋から南約1kmの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == 関連項目 == {{commons|Category:Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ; 猿田彦大神を祀る神社 * [[椿大神社]] - 伊勢国一宮を称するもう1つの神社。 * [[猿田彦神社]] * [[白鬚神社]] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つはきしんしやなかとしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社1]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]]

It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were amalgamated in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto'''|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|'''Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto'''| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Jinja'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were amalgamated in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto'''|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|'''Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto'''| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

revision.comment: Dekimasu moved page [[Tsubaki shrine]] to [[Tsubaki Jinja]] over redirect: this should probably be "Tsubaki Shrine", but a move to that title was already reverted under [[WP:RMUM]], so moves like the one to the lowercase title should not be treated as uncontroversial

Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. 都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Jinja'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto'''|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|'''Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto'''| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. [[伊勢国]][[一宮]]
旧[[県社]] |創建 = （伝）[[雄略天皇]]23年 |例祭 = [[10月10日]] |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=4.5|lon_deg=136|lon_min=36|lon_sec=4.5|label=都波岐神社
奈加等神社|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}} }} [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|鳥居]] '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]。 == 概要 == 両社とも[[式内社]]で、[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 [[明治]]時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、'''都波岐奈加等神社'''とも称される。 == 祭神 == ; 都波岐神社 * '''[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]''' （さるたひこのおおかみ） ; 奈加等神社 * '''天椹野命''' （あまのくののみこと） - 中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖。 * '''[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]''' （なかつつおのみこと） == 歴史 == 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年、[[勅]]により[[伊勢国造]]の高雄束命[[猿田彦]]の八世の孫とされる。が[[伊勢国]][[河曲郡]][[中跡村]]（現在地）に社殿を2つ造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが起源とされる。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』では両社とも小社に列した。[[天長]]年間に[[空海|弘法大師（空海）]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを[[奉納]]した。[[承暦]]3年（[[1079年]]）、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より[[宸翰|宸筆]]の[[勅額]]が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際に社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物が再建した。 [[明治]]時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として[[県社]]に列格。現在は2社の[[祭神]]が1つの社殿に[[祭神#主神・配神|相殿]]として祀られる。 == 主な祭事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 所在地 == * [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 == 交通アクセス == ; 路線バス * [[河原田駅]]（[[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[関西線 (名古屋地区)|関西本線]]・[[伊勢鉄道]][[伊勢鉄道伊勢線|伊勢線]]）または[[鈴鹿市駅]]（[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄鈴鹿線|鈴鹿線]]）から、[[三重交通]]バスで「高岡」バス停下車、徒歩で約10分。 ; 自家用車 * [[国道23号]]鈴鹿大橋から南約1kmの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == 関連項目 == {{commons|Category:Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ; 猿田彦大神を祀る神社 * [[椿大神社]] - 伊勢国一宮を称するもう1つの神社。 * [[猿田彦神社]] * [[白鬚神社]] {{神道 横}} {{一宮}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つはきしんしやなかとしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社1]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]]

The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia31]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia31]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia31]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia24]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia24]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Images]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ise Province]] [[category:kk]][[category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ichinomiya]] [[category:kk]][[category:Fuken-sha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ise Province]] [[category:kk]][[category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ichinomiya]] [[category:kk]][[category:Fuken-sha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ise Province]] [[category:kk]][[category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ichinomiya]] [[category:kk]][[category:Fuken-sha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ise Province]] [[category:kk]][[category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ichinomiya]] [[category:kk]][[category:Fuken-sha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe.
