{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[category:Ise Province]] [[category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[category:Ichinomiya]] [[category:Fuken-sha]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

[[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]]

It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] {{Ichinomiya}} [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} ]] ]] ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court Ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages to be overwritten]] }} [[sn:Tsubaki Shrine]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

{{出典の明記|date=2019年10月}} {{神社 |名称 = 都波岐神社・奈加等神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg|250px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|54|04.5|N|136|36|04.5|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=都波岐神社・奈加等神社}} |祭神 = 都波岐神社：[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]
奈加等神社：天椹野命、[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]] |社格 = [[式内社]]（小2社）
[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]
旧[[県社]] |創建 = （伝）[[雄略天皇]]23年 |例祭 = [[10月10日]] |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=4.5|lon_deg=136|lon_min=36|lon_sec=4.5|label=都波岐神社
奈加等神社|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}} }} [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|鳥居]] '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]。 == 概要 == 両社とも[[式内社]]で、[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 [[明治]]時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、'''都波岐奈加等神社'''とも称される。 == 祭神 == ; 都波岐神社 * '''[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]''' （さるたひこのおおかみ） ; 奈加等神社 * '''天椹野命''' （あまのくののみこと） - 中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖。 * '''[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]''' （なかつつおのみこと） == 歴史 == 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年、[[勅]]により[[伊勢国造]]の高雄束命[[猿田彦]]の八世の孫とされる。が[[伊勢国]][[河曲郡]][[中跡村]]（現在地）に社殿を2つ造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが起源とされる。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』では両社とも小社に列した。[[天長]]年間に[[空海|弘法大師（空海）]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを[[奉納]]した。[[承暦]]3年（[[1079年]]）、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より[[宸翰|宸筆]]の[[勅額]]が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際に社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物が再建した。 [[明治]]時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として[[県社]]に列格。現在は2社の[[祭神]]が1つの社殿に[[祭神#主神・配神|相殿]]として祀られる。 == 主な祭事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 所在地 == * [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 == 交通アクセス == ; 路線バス * [[河原田駅]]（[[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[関西線 (名古屋地区)|関西本線]]・[[伊勢鉄道]][[伊勢鉄道伊勢線|伊勢線]]）または[[鈴鹿市駅]]（[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄鈴鹿線|鈴鹿線]]）から、[[三重交通]]バスで「高岡」バス停下車、徒歩で約10分。 ; 自家用車 * [[国道23号]]鈴鹿大橋から南約1kmの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == 関連項目 == {{commons|Category:Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ; 猿田彦大神を祀る神社 * [[椿大神社]] - 伊勢国一宮を称するもう1つの神社。 * [[猿田彦神社]] * [[白鬚神社]] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つはきしんしやなかとしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社1]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]]

拝殿 |所在地 = [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|54|04.5|N|136|36|04.5|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=都波岐神社・奈加等神社}} |祭神 = 都波岐神社：[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]

revision.comment: /* 交通アクセス */ [[Wikipedia:表記ガイド#単位]]「単位は原則として記号を用いず、かな・漢字で表記します。例：m → メートル、 kg → キログラム」に基づき、「1km」→「1キロメートル」に修正。
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2019年10月}} {{神社 |名称 = 都波岐神社・奈加等神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg|250px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|54|04.5|N|136|36|04.5|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=都波岐神社・奈加等神社}} |祭神 = 都波岐神社：[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]
奈加等神社：天椹野命、[[住吉三神|中筒之男命]] |社格 = [[式内社]]（小）
[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]
旧[[県社]] |創建 = （伝）[[雄略天皇]]23年 |例祭 = [[10月10日]] |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=4.5|lon_deg=136|lon_min=36|lon_sec=4.5|label=都波岐神社
奈加等神社|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}} }} [[ファイル:Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|鳥居]] '''都波岐神社・奈加等神社'''（つばきじんじゃ・なかとじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]にある[[神社]]。 == 概要 == 両社とも[[式内社]]で、[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]。都波岐神社は[[伊勢国]][[一宮]]とされる。 [[明治]]時代に都波岐神社と奈加等神社を合併したもので、'''都波岐奈加等神社'''とも称される。 == 祭神 == ; 都波岐神社 * [[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]（さるたひこのおおかみ） ; 奈加等神社 * 天椹野命（あまのくののみこと） - 中跡直（なかとのあたい）の先祖。 * [[住吉三神|中筒之男命]]（なかつつおのみこと） == 歴史 == 社伝によれば、[[雄略天皇]]23年、[[勅]]により[[伊勢国造]]の高雄束命[[猿田彦]]の八世の孫とされる。が[[伊勢国]][[河曲郡]][[中跡村]]（現在地）に社殿を2つ造営し、それぞれ都波岐神社・奈加等神社と称したのが起源とされる。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』では両社とも小社に列した。[[天長]]年間に[[空海|弘法大師（空海）]]が参籠し、[[獅子頭]]2つを[[奉納]]した。[[承暦]]3年（[[1079年]]）、2社に対して[[正一位]]の[[神階]]が授けられ、[[白河天皇]]より[[宸翰|宸筆]]の[[勅額]]が授けられた。[[永禄]]年間、[[織田信長]]の伊勢平定の際に社殿を焼失し、旧記も失われた。[[寛永]]年間、[[神戸城]]主一柳監物が再建した。 [[明治]]時代に両社をあわせて「都波岐神社奈加等神社」として[[県社]]に列格。現在は2社の[[祭神]]が1つの社殿に[[祭神#主神・配神|相殿]]として祀られる。 == 主な祭事 == * [[例大祭]]、中戸[[獅子舞]]（10月10日） == 所在地 == * [[三重県]][[鈴鹿市]]一ノ宮町1181 == 交通アクセス == ; 路線バス * [[河原田駅]]（[[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[関西線 (名古屋地区)|関西本線]]・[[伊勢鉄道]][[伊勢鉄道伊勢線|伊勢線]]）または[[鈴鹿市駅]]（[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄鈴鹿線|鈴鹿線]]）から、[[三重交通]]バスで「高岡」バス停下車、徒歩で約10分。 ; 自家用車 * [[国道23号]]鈴鹿大橋から南約1キロメートルの一ノ宮町[[交差点]]近く。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == 関連項目 == {{commons|Category:Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ; 猿田彦大神を祀る神社 * [[椿大神社]] - 伊勢国一宮を称するもう1つの神社。 * [[猿田彦神社]] * [[白鬚神社]] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つはきしんしやなかとしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社1]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Suzuka, Mie, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsubaki Shrine
都波岐神社 | image = Tsubaki-jinja Shaden1.jpg | caption = ''Shaden'' of Tsubaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|4.5|N|136|36|4.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | founded_by = c.[[Emperor Yūryaku]] | established = | date_destroyed = | location =1181 Ichinomiya-chō, Suzuka-shi, Mie-ken 513-0031 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki Shrine'''|都波岐神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Suzuka, Mie|Suzuka]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ise Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on October 10. It is also known as the {{nihongo|'''Tsubaki-Nakato Jinja'''|都波岐奈加等神社||}}, as the shrine consisted of two separate shrines, the Tsubaki Jinja and the Nakato Jinja, which were [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|amalgamated]] in the [[Meiji period]].{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tsubaki Shrine is: * {{nihongo|[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]| 玉祖命|}}, leader of the ''[[kunitsukami]]'' and patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]]. The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine's legend states that it was founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (reigned 456 to 479). Both shrines first appear in historical documentation in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and are classed as "minor shrines". [[Kūkai]] is alleged to have visited the shrine, and to have donated two masks for the [[lion dance]]. It is uncertain also when it began to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, although this is contested by [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]], (also in Suzuka); however by 1079 it had advanced to the highest court ranking, and was made a shrine for prayers to the imperial family by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. However, the shrine was razed by [[Oda Nobunaga]] during his conquest of Ise Province and all old records were lost. The shrine was revived by the Hitotsuyanagi clan, [[daimyō]] of [[Kanbe Domain]] during the early [[Edo Period]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a Prefectural shrine in 1871 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tsubaki-jinja Torii.jpg|Entry with Torii Tsubaki-jinja Shaden2.jpg|Shaden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the other Ise Ichinomiya ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tsubaki Nakato-jinja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Province]] [[Category:Suzuka, Mie]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tsubaki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Tsubaki Shrine]]

The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakato Shrine are: * {{nihongo|Ama-no-kuno-no-Mikoto|天椹野命|}}, ancestor of the Nakato clan * {{nihongo|Nakatsutsuo-no-mikoto| 中筒之男命 |}}, one of the [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] ==History== The origins of Tsubaki Shrine are unknown.
